Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As an 87-year-old man living in Los Angeles, retired and with a life full of experiences, I see fake content on social media as a troubling issue that requires careful handling. Having immigrated here and raised three children, now widowed, I value honesty and community, shaped by my Christian beliefs and good mental health. I use social media a few times weekly to stay connected with family and friends, sharing my hobbies like cycling, baking, cooking, and photography. My personality traits—high extraversion and agreeableness, moderate conscientiousness and neuroticism, and low openness—mean I enjoy social interactions but am cautious about new things. To manage fake content, I rely on common sense and trusted sources. When I see something suspicious, I check with my children or close friends before sharing, as I believe in protecting others from misinformation. I avoid engaging with sensational posts and focus on positive, uplifting content that aligns with my values. Social media platforms should do more to filter out false information, but as users, we have a responsibility too. By being skeptical and not spreading unverified claims, we can help maintain a truthful online environment. My approach is simple: think before you click, and always prioritize kindness and integrity in your digital interactions.",

Combined, this leads to an opinion that values community, skepticism, and personal responsibility in managing fake content, avoiding technical solutions in favor of human judgment and ethical behavior.",
  "tone": "Warm, cautious, community-oriented, and practical, with a focus on personal responsibility and kindness, reflecting high agreeableness and extraversion, low openness, and life experience.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As an 87-year-old man living in Los Angeles, retired and with a life full of experiences, I see fake content on social media as a troubling issue that requires careful handling. Having immigrated here and raised three children, now widowed, I value honesty and community, shaped by my Christian beliefs and good mental health. I use social media a few times weekly to stay connected with family and friends, sharing my hobbies like cycling, baking, cooking, and photography. My personality traits—high extraversion and agreeableness, moderate conscientiousness and neuroticism, and low openness—mean I enjoy social interactions but am cautious about new things. To manage fake content, I rely on common sense and trusted sources. When I see something suspicious, I check with my children or close friends before sharing, as I believe in protecting others from misinformation. I avoid engaging with sensational posts and focus on positive, uplifting content that aligns with my values. Social media platforms should do more to filter out false information, but as users, we have a responsibility too. By being skeptical and not spreading unverified claims, we can help maintain a truthful online environment. My approach is simple: think before you click, and always prioritize kindness and integrity in your digital interactions.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age (87) suggests wisdom and caution; gender and sexual orientation not heavily influential; occupation (retired) and city (Los Angeles) imply leisure time and urban exposure; education (lower secondary) indicates reliance on practical experience over formal training; marital status (widowed) and children (3) highlight family focus; migration status (immigrant) adds perspective on adaptation; psychological feature (good mental health) supports balanced view; religious beliefs (Christianity) emphasize honesty; time on social media (few times weekly) shows moderate use; hobbies (cycling, baking, etc.) suggest active, creative lifestyle; OCEAN traits: low openness (cautious about new info), moderate conscientiousness (some organization), high extraversion (social engagement), high agreeableness (cooperative, kind), moderate neuroticism (stable emotions). Combined, this leads to an opinion that values community, skepticism, and personal responsibility in managing fake content, avoiding technical solutions in favor of human judgment and ethical behavior.
response_parsed.tone: Warm, cautious, community-oriented, and practical, with a focus on personal responsibility and kindness, reflecting high agreeableness and extraversion, low openness, and life experience.
